title: Building belonging in medical education
abstract: Belonging is increasingly recognised as an important component of successful medical education. It affects academic achievement, wellbeing, and professional identity formation. This article examines how this concept applies to undergraduate students on community clinical placements and postgraduate vocational general practice (GP) trainees. Additionally, we consider the broader impact of belonging beyond the learner–on patient safety and quality of care. We explore how structural, cultural and relational factors can promote or hinder a sense of belonging. The shift towards competency-based training, reductions in continuity of care, and changes in the primary care infrastructure and workforce, complicate traditional notions of belonging. This highlights the need for holistic, flexible and inclusive educational practices. Key themes that promote belonging include the importance of connection through peer networks, mentorship, continuity, an inclusive learning environment which fosters psychological safety, and learner autonomy to accommodate differing needs and interests. Forming high quality relationships strengthens all of these. We propose centring a sense of belonging in medical education through a more relational approach. This can better support the development of resilient, reflective and connected general practitioners prepared to meet the evolving needs of primary care.

LEADING ARTICLE
Building belonging in medical education
Shabana Bharmala and Sanjiv Ahluwaliab
aDepartment of Community based medical education, Queen Mary University of London, London, UK; bSchool of Medicine, Anglia Ruskin 
University, Chelmsford, UK
ABSTRACT
Belonging is increasingly recognised as an important component of successful medical education. 
It affects academic achievement, wellbeing, and professional identity formation. This article 
examines how this concept applies to undergraduate students on community clinical placements 
and postgraduate vocational general practice (GP) trainees. Additionally, we consider the broader 
impact of belonging beyond the learner – on patient safety and quality of care. We explore how 
structural, cultural and relational factors can promote or hinder a sense of belonging. The shift 
towards competency-based training, reductions in continuity of care, and changes in the primary 
care infrastructure and workforce, complicate traditional notions of belonging. This highlights the 
need for holistic, flexible and inclusive educational practices. Key themes that promote belonging 
include the importance of connection through peer networks, mentorship, continuity, an inclusive 
learning environment which fosters psychological safety, and learner autonomy to accommodate 
differing needs and interests. Forming high quality relationships strengthens all of these. We 
propose centring a sense of belonging in medical education through a more relational approach. 
This can better support the development of resilient, reflective and connected general practi­
tioners prepared to meet the evolving needs of primary care.
ARTICLE HISTORY 
Received 17 April 2025  
Revised 14 May 2025  
Accepted 24 May 2025 
KEYWORDS 
Medical education; 
Belonging; primary care; 
relational; connection
Introduction
The traditional route to a career in general practice, 
focused on a vocational experience between GP trainee 
and GP trainer followed by entry into a partnership, has 
been steadily challenged over the past 15 years. Factors 
include moves towards competency-based training, pres­
sures of healthcare commodification and business man­
agement in general practice, and diminishing resources at 
a time of rising demand. These have all contributed to 
changes in the primary care workforce, reductions in 
continuity of care, and an increasing sense of dis- 
satisfaction with patient experience of general practice.
We now work in more diverse teams, with a much 
greater mixed economy of types of professionals who 
have a wider variety of working arrangements – this is 
the change that our patients, students, trainees and staff 
are experiencing. Reductions in continuity of care and its 
impact on patient outcomes make it necessary for us to 
reflect on the consequences of the changes wrought upon 
the clinical workforce in general practice. One response 
would be to argue for a rollback of the many changes 
implemented in the commissioning, delivery, and train­
ing of general practice over the last 20 years. This is 
neither feasible nor necessarily desirable. Another is to 
consider the future of general practice (and its workforce) 
through the values that really matter. One of these 
(amongst many) is the sense of ‘belonging’ that patients 
and staff describe as being important. In this article, we 
therefore explore the meaning of belonging in general 
practice and its education, factors that affect this, and 
what this might mean for the future of medical education.
What is belonging in the context of general 
practice?
Belonging is a complex and multi-dimensional concept 
which lacks a single agreed-upon definition [1]. The 
psychologists Baumeister and Leary identified belong­
ing as a fundamental need where human beings are 
deeply motivated to form and maintain ‘strong, stable 
interpersonal relationships’ [2]. Focusing on the learn­
ing environment, the educational literature builds on 
this concept with emphasis on the experience of feeling 
accepted, valued, included and supported by others 
[3,4]. This sense of connection is dynamic, occurring 
in overlapping academic, social, personal and physical 
spaces. It goes beyond feeling connected to peers, to also 
include staff and the wider learning environment and is

shaped by contextual factors, past experiences and men­
tal health [5]. In health professional education, Levett- 
Jones and Lathlean added that the degree to which an 
individual’s professional and/or personal values are in 
harmony with those of the group is a key factor [6].
Belonging in medical education can be fostered 
across different levels: to a specific clinical rotation, to 
the wider academic community of the medical school or 
vocational training scheme (VTS), or at the broadest 
level, to the medical profession. In the context of the 
primary care clinical placement, an individual’s sense of 
belonging is intrinsically linked to their experiential 
learning experiences of patient encounters, observing 
role models, practising their skills, receiving persona­
lised feedback and reflecting on these activities. The 
organisational culture, interactions with people and 
the environment, in addition to individual level factors 
such as the learner’s affective attributes all shape the 
extent to which it is felt.
Why is this important?
Belonging has a positive psychological impact [2]. It is 
linked with positive mental state, cognitive engagement 
and resilience. Conversely when there is a lack of 
belonging, through isolation or social exclusion, it 
results in negative psychological, behavioural, physical 
and mental health consequences [2].
These impacts shape the ability to engage with the 
learning process and influence academic outcomes. 
Learners with a greater sense of belonging are associated 
with higher levels of academic motivation [7], engage­
ment [8], and improved academic success and reten­
tion [9].
Additionally, belonging plays an important role in 
professional identity formation, particularly for diverse 
identities and voices [10]. Mayer et al. highlight that for 
disabled healthcare learners, the social context and the 
legitimisation of an individual’s professional role by the 
community of practice are crucial [11]. This is fostered 
through a supportive and inclusive environment which 
values the richness of diverse representation.
From an organisational perspective, staff working in 
a healthcare environment where they feel that they are 
valued, trusted, and can safely raise concerns, leads to 
higher-quality care and patient outcomes [12].
Factors influencing a sense of belonging in the 
NHS
The NHS faces numerous challenges that impact its 
ability to meet core needs to build belonging. There 
are ongoing workforce retention challenges with notable 
stress and burnout in doctors [13]. Recent reports have 
highlighted the widespread issues of discrimination 
across the healthcare environment [14].
As such, it requires ongoing effort to foster and 
maintain belonging, ensuring that learners feel sup­
ported and engaged throughout their educational jour­
ney. Compassionate leadership can help promote an 
inclusive, person-centred culture that prioritises the 
fundamental needs of staff, which includes fostering 
belonging [15].
Psychological safety in the clinical learning environ­
ment – where individuals within a group feel able to 
take interpersonal risk and contribute without fear of 
negative consequences – increases learners’ sense of 
belonging [16]. McClintock et al. identify that psycho­
logical safety is often lacking because of the systems and 
structures in the healthcare environment [16]. At the 
organisational level, the fast pace of work and high 
productivity pressures can erode into protected time 
for development or perceived support for work-life bal­
ance. Social hierarchy and power differentials within 
teams can adversely affect building relationships with 
teams and social cohesion. Observable behaviours that 
can promote psychological safety and, in turn, a sense of 
belonging include leadership being physically available, 
respectful interpersonal communications and providing 
learner autonomy.
Broader contextual matters may also influence the 
sense of belonging. The framing of the healthcare sys­
tem as ‘broken’, a focus on efficiency and productivity 
over other aspects such as quality of care, and the 
burden of bureaucracy can all have the potential to 
form barriers to belonging.
Transactional and relational interactions in 
healthcare
According to Iles, interactions in healthcare can be 
broadly classified into transactional and relational ones 
[17]. Transactional interactions are characterised by 
formalised processes, standardised protocols, and 
a focus on measurable outcomes. Iles observed that 
healthcare has shifted from being a ‘covenant of care’ 
to a series of ‘auditable transactions’, where healthcare 
interactions are reduced to quantifiable exchanges. This 
model emphasises efficiency and accountability but 
under-values the individuality of patients and profes­
sional judgement of healthcare providers.
By contrast, relational interactions prioritise the 
quality of relationships among healthcare professionals 
and between caregivers and patients. Iles advocates for 
recognising the importance of these relationships in 
delivering compassionate and effective care. She 
EDUCATION FOR PRIMARY CARE
195
suggests that nurturing strong interpersonal connec­
tions within healthcare teams can lead to improved 
patient outcomes and a more supportive work 
environment.
It is our contention that both high quality transac­
tional and relational interactions are required in health­
care. However, the focus on transactional interactions 
(thought to be easier to observe and measure) risks 
crowding out the benefits of relational interactions.
Importance of a relational approach to 
belonging in medical education
A relational approach to medical education priori­
tises mentorship, peer support, and professional inte­
gration (see Table 1). These foster a sense of 
belonging and identity formation among medical 
students and trainees. Interventions such as longitu­
dinal supervision, structured mentorship schemes, 
peer support networks, and transition programmes 
have been shown to enhance trainee confidence and 
well-being. The Vocational Training Scheme (VTS) 
provides structured peer learning, while initiatives 
like buddy schemes for GP trainees and the RCGP 
First5 programme offer informal mentorship and 
ongoing 
professional 
support. 
Similarly, 
Longitudinal Integrated Clerkships (LICs) in under­
graduate education strengthen early professional 
identity by maintaining sustained exposure to gen­
eral practice.
In contrast, transactional approaches focus on 
procedural training, formalised evaluation and stan­
dardised 
competency 
assessments 
such 
as 
Workplace-Based Assessments (WPBA), the Applied 
Knowledge Test (AKT), and Multi-Source Feedback 
(MSF). While essential for evaluating skills, these 
assessments do not actively foster professional 
belonging and often fail to address the social and 
emotional dimensions of professional identity forma­
tion. GP trainees, in secondary care placements, may 
lack 
integration 
into 
hospital 
teams, 
with 
a diminished sense of connection to the broader 
medical workforce.
Promoting a sense of belonging
Fostering 
belonging 
requires 
institution-wide 
approaches where everyone in the learning community – 
learners, educators, practice staff and organisational 
leadership – has a key role.
Opportunities such as the induction period can be 
developed to promote belonging by establishing 
a strong foundation of quality relationships with peers, 
educators and practice staff. Example activities include 
preparing across the practice team to create 
a welcoming environment and sharing key information 
about the objectives of the placement. Spending time 
individually with learners at the start of the placement to 
explore their expectations and discuss potential chal­
lenges can help develop a mutual understanding. This 
can promote student agency and support tailoring high- 
impact learning opportunities.
Effective educator practices include promoting 
opportunities for learners to build connections. This 
could include designing small group activities in an 
active learning environment to facilitate formal inter­
actions as well as having regular breaks in a social 
space for informal discussions. Additionally, using 
learner names as much as possible can have 
a powerful effect.
Promoting inclusivity through modelling inclusive 
behaviour and attitudes, and having an explicit social 
contract of respect helps to facilitate an environment 
where all learners feel valued.
Table 1. Interventions across undergraduate community clinical placement and postgraduate GP training.
Relational
Transactional
Vocational Training Scheme (VTS) – Peer support groups
Workplace-Based Assessments (WPBA) – Competency evaluations
Structured mentorship programmes – Formal mentoring by experienced GPs
Applied Knowledge Test (AKT) – Medical knowledge assessment
Buddy schemes for GP trainees – Near-peer support
Multi-Source Feedback (MSF) – Performance review based on colleague 
feedback
RCGP First5 initiative – Peer support and mentorship for newly qualified GPs
ePortfolio and competency sign-offs – Administrative tracking of 
training progress
Longitudinal Integrated Clerkships (LICs) – Continuous GP exposure during 
undergraduate education
Limited integration in hospital-based and GP placements
Practice-based learning and team integration – Active immersion in clinical 
practice
ARCP panel assessments – Formal review of training progress
Student selected modules and electives - Providing learner autonomy and 
accommodating flexible needs/interests
Objective structured clinical exam (OSCE) – skills assessment
Institutional focus on providing an equitable experience for students from diverse 
backgrounds
Institutional focus on the numbers of students from widening 
participation and diverse backgrounds
University academic societies - Building a community of practice
196
S. BHARMAL AND S. AHLUWALIA
Efforts focused on these areas can help learners to feel 
confident to contribute, increase their sense of belong­
ing, and achieve their full potential.
Conclusion
Through a holistic approach to medical education, 
belonging can be fostered across different levels and 
social spaces that each build towards the sense of con­
nection, inclusion and support. Having meaningful 
patient encounters and collaborating with peers and 
the practice staff in a psychologically safe learning envir­
onment where their contributions are valued helps pro­
mote a sense of purpose.
We propose that a culture shift towards more rela­
tional medical education can foster a greater sense of 
belonging. This provides a new perspective to address 
the current medical education and workforce 
challenges.
